speaker: interviewer
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: January 6, 1997. Yvonne de Vries interviewing Stan Rubens in Honolulu, Hawaii, United States of America. And the interview will be in the English language. My name is Yvonne de Vries, and I'm interviewing Stan Rubens.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Stan Rubens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-A-N, and the last name, R-U-B-- for boy-- E-N-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born November 16, 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the city of Enschede in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-N-S-C-H-E-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you explain us where this city is located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enschede is located very close to the German border, on the eastern part in the middle of the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, at the moment, 64.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell us the first memories you have when you were a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess one of the first memories I, I have from when my father had a store, a retail store, in, in the city of Enschede, and I stood on top of the stairs, which was a double stairs. And I-- there was a little gate there for me not to go down the stairs. Of course, I managed to open the gate and fell promptly down the stairs. That's the only memory I have from Enschede. And thereafter, we moved to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the year, but-- '32, '34, '35, '36, '37-- I think it must have been about '37 or '38, 1937 or '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in Enschede?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never went to school in Enschede. Because I was four years old or four and 1/2 years old when I left Enschede.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go to kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't go to kindergarten there. That I did in-- when I was in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, his name was Jacob Paul Rubens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in-- how to call that? I guess best explained as he had a retail store in glassware, chinaware, crystal, leather goods, custom jewelry, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did this store have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the store had a name. It was called Rubens [DUTCH], which translated to mean Rubens, the House of Gifts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of man was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was-- actually, I think my father was a troubled person, quite troubled with many things. He and I, we had very little communications. If I would ask him something like, how does this work or what about this? He would say, well, you're a little bit, little bit too young for that. I'll tell you that when you grow up-- when you grow up more.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did not have much communication. He was reasonably kind, I would think, though he was very quick with his hands to slap. And I guess I, I grew up in an atmosphere of uncertainty and a lot of tension because of my father's attitude towards the family. And who was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is Eva Themans. And do you want me to spell that? T-H-E-M-A-N-S. And she was born in Enschede, and where my parents met and lived for a while till they moved to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason they moved to Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my father felt that it was a economic better situation for him with-- to have a store in Amsterdam than to have a store in Enschede.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the reason--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was for economic reasons that he did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of person was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, my, mother, yeah-- how do I describe my mother? Both my parents were always working. So I didn't see that much of them. Later on, when I look back at them, I have a feeling that they were-- how they found each other is, to me, a riddle. Because each one of them was usually very selfish towards their own, own objectives and what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was hard to, hard to say what kind of people they were. I think they were-- this was caused by the, the way they grew up. They were an, an outcome of the, of the time of life, which was very difficult for them. I think they had a great amount of difficulty. For just a kid, I could, of course-- I would not understand. Could you explain those difficulties?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they had financial difficulties to, to cope and deal with making life as good as possible for themselves. I think that was the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your relationship with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's-- my relationship with my mother and I was fine. We had no difficulties. And that-- the reason why, in retrospect, I, I, I feel sometimes I was kind of being played in between. Because my father would not hesitate to hit my mother. And I rebelled in the strongest fashion against that and literally, would jump in between the two of them and stop them from fighting. So the result was that my mother, of course, was very kind to me. And my father sometimes was ready to kick me in the [NON-ENGLISH] Because I, I was interfering with his macho position.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of work was your mother doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my mother worked with my father in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was taking care of you when they were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a little kid, my-- I had a governess, I guess you would call it, who would come in at about 9 o'clock and take me to kindergarten. And we were staying at a pension, where the food was ready when my parents came home. And they had a couple of rooms there for my sister, who was a few years older than I am. And we lived there for-- 'til about 1939, for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the-- what is now called the [? Churchillaan. ?] It had another name before. I can't remember that name at that time. And thereafter, we moved to the Apollolaan, number 47, where we had-- were at a ground floor. And that's, that's where we moved. By then, I was about six years old. And my, my parents thought it was quite all right, when I was six years old, for me to walk to school, which was about a, maybe a mile and a half. And the [NON-ENGLISH] from the Apollolaan, and they would go out and leave me in charge of the house. So I guess, basically, I became very independent at a very early age.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were mentioning you had a sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Ronny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe your, your relationship with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, we cared very much for each other. It was a, it was, and is still, a very good relationship, by which we are always very concerned for each other's well being.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person is she? Can you describe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I describe her, I would say that she is-- she was-- when she was very young, she was extremely, extremely impatient. She was so impatient, there are no words to describe that. Everything-- she had a very volatile character. What's that lady who was married to-- who speaks with a very terrible Mexican accent [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, come on. She plays here in Hawaii also-- who comes across as a very emotional, volatile person. And that was my, and that was and is my sister. She's quieted down maybe just a little bit, but very, very emotional, very emotional, and still is, basically speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you grow up in a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not religious at all. We maybe went to synagogue once a year for Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were conscious of the fact that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very conscious of the fact that I was Jewish. And when I was, oh, about six to seven years old, I even went to Sunday school for a very short time, until the war broke out and this became totally impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you moved to Amsterdam, Hitler already was in power in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you hear people talking about this? And your-- did your parents talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they did, I was too young to understand it. Because I was only six years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school at [INAUDIBLE] in Amsterdam, first and second year. But in the second year, I went to another school which was closer to, closer to the house where I was living. And by then, I think the war had already broken out. It was 1940 by then. So 19-- early-- late 1940 or-- yeah, I think in September of 1940, I went to that other school already, to my second grade preliminary school or elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have the store in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it attached to the place you were living--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was somewhere else. That was [INAUDIBLE], Let me give that in English. [NON-ENGLISH] 106, 108.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to the store frequently?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, yes-- usually after school, I would go to the, to the store, because my father wanted me to work in the store. As young as I was, he had always things for me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a lot of Jewish customers coming to the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I do not recall at all. There was a fair amount of Jewish people, of course, in those days living in Amsterdam. There was a big, a big Jewish community in Amsterdam, very big. Of course, most of them disappeared during the Nazi occupation and, and, and the aftermath of that and the result of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But yes, there was a very big Jewish community. And there was even a ghetto-like area in Amsterdam. And occasionally, I, I would go there with my father or maybe it was a family member. And I would be so amazed as a child about, why would these people live here in this area as uncomfortable, in a way, as could be? And I counted my blessings I was not living there. Could you explain a little bit more about how the people were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that would be difficult for me to do because of my-- I was only six, seven years old, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But what, what impressed you about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What impressed me was the, the Yiddish speaking, which, strangely enough, I didn't really care that much for. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was totally unaware of-- you see, my, my parents never paid much attention to me. And maybe it was that when I was in that area, that they paid a lot more attention to children than my parents paid to me. And it almost felt embarrassing to me. Maybe that was the reason. There was much more kindness, actually, in the ghetto, though it wasn't a closed off ghetto like in other cities. It was a total open area, but the area where a lot of Jewish people would congregate. And I think the only reason why we came there, because on occasion, maybe we wanted some kosher meat. Maybe that's the only reason how I got there. I still can totally picture, as young as I was at the time, the butchery shop where we would buy some meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not much to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just one open window, and an open space with a counter, and the butchers-- two or three butchers-- would be standing behind it. Very simple, very simple-- some butcher blocks and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us before that you, you were amazed that people were living in, in certain circumstances over there. Were the people very poor, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, very difficult for me to realize how poor they were, or if they were poor. Everybody seemed well. And everybody seemed in a reasonable good mood. But the typical Jewishness, I was not used to it. And because of this typical Jewishness, the, the, the dialects, the, the very Jewish words that were being spoken, the Yiddish that was being spoken, it was not an, an, an area of comfort for me. I didn't feel that comfortable with it. And strangely enough, if I use-- sometimes when I talk to my daughter and I use a Yiddish expression, she will look at me and say, oh, come on, Dad. Knock it off. She doesn't like it, either. And I immediately translate that when I was in her shoes or at her age or whatever, I didn't like it either. And somehow, this seems to have carried through in my daughter also. She's not interested in the, the typical Jewish expressions or the, the, the Yiddish things, even though I sometimes-- I've learned over the years, of course, a fair amount of Yiddish words. But I, I don't use them. I use them very sparingly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it's too much to ask you, if you, if you now can explain why this feeling came about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, why-- you have a feeling you don't want to be-- don't-- you feel uncomfortable with this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe there was an electricity in the air. I am very sensitive to, to, to the way a, a room-- I can walk into a house, into a room, into an area. Any area I walk into, I pick up vibrations. That's the word I was looking for. And the moment I pick up those vibrations, they get translated into good, bad, evil, medium, danger, watch out. I get all this in-- just in a flash. It comes to me whether this is right or wrong. And it has helped me in my business in later years, where I, I can walk into a, into an establishment and immediately sense if this place is doing well, doing bad, mediocre, if there has been a fight, if there has been arguments, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I felt there was a certain tension in the ghetto. And the tension made me feel uncomfortable. I didn't feel good about it. Does that, does that give you an idea? Right. So, you didn't grow up in a Jewish environment, or did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really did not. And I'll, I'll remember when, when my grandmother-- because my grandmother on my mother's side was very liberal. And my grandmother on my father's side was not so liberal. So when I went to my grandmother for vacation and I ate some butter or milk with meat and I said, am I allowed to, to do that?

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, yeah, that's quite all right. I said, well, is that not religious, not allowed? And she said, don't worry about it. And, so I didn't worry about it. The schools you went to, were they Jewish schools or public?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not Jewish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, I, I-- well, not so much when the war broke out, but when the Germans came into, into Amsterdam. And I, I still see myself, ridiculous as it may seem, as a little boy standing at the corner in front of the Apollo Hall-- at that time, I think they still have ice skating there-- and, and the corner of the Apollolaan. And I happen to-- just happen to be there. I was always roaming around, going places. Nothing seemed to scare me, really, to go anywhere, as a little kid even. And I, I was standing at the corner. And the German occupation came into, into Amsterdam. And some of the guys were waving to me. And I kind of waved back. And then almost like my other hand wanted to grab my hand down, don't do that. These are not good people you want to wave to. But I, I will never forget that particular scene, how I was waving to the Germans and saying to myself, I'm nuts. I shouldn't be waving to, to them. That's the enemy. Yeah, that's, that's, that's how I remember the first Germans coming to, to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents explain about what the Nazis did to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never did, never did-- the only thing was that I think children pick up the underlying fear from their parents immediately. A little child will starts-- will start to cry when a mother starts to cry. It seems to transfer itself automatically. I think children, of course, are very sensitive to, also to vibrations in the air. So in, in this case, I was picking up the vibrations in the air, and said, oh boy. We are in deep trouble. And then my father started to explain to me that we may have to go into hiding or underground from the, from the Nazis. Because they were-- and he said, when I was in Germany, and he had lived for many years in Germany, and he spoke fluent German. He spoke so good German that, German that he could pass for German, as a matter of fact. He, he said, well, he said, this is going the wrong way. We are going to be in, in big difficulties. And like, he bought a lot of food when the war broke out, like a lot of people did in Amsterdam, or all over, to cover themselves in case of shortages. I still don't care that much for tomato soup with rice because of the hundreds of cans of tomato soup and rice we had. But I, I felt thoroughly, completely, that this was difficult times. And I knew we were at war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was somewhere around about October of '41, or maybe September. It was still very warm. And my father-- it was a warm time. Must have been September. My father said, OK, he said, put on about three, four times the underwear that you normally wear and as much clothing as you can without looking bulky. Because you can't take a suitcase with you. And we, we going to go to another place. But we're just going to walk there like hey, nothing is happening. He had a car. And-- but the car was taken away from him. He had a store, but a [INAUDIBLE], as they called it, somebody walked into the store and said to him, hey, [INAUDIBLE], Jew, Rubens, get out. I am taking over your store. And he had barely a chance to grab his coat and his hat to, to get out of the store without being literally kicked out of the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I absolutely do not remember that, because my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a German or a Dutch person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Dutch person. Yeah, it was a Dutch person. You know, there were a lot of good Dutch people. But also, there were quite a bit of bad Dutch people in the-- during the war time. You know all, all about that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, like, in 1941, you went into hiding. The war broke out in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the, the year in between, what changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you may know, the Germans were very subtle about their prosecution event. It reminds me of the story of the frog that is being boiled in a kettle by a Frenchman. And the, the water is, is kind of cool. And then the Frenchman builds his fire underneath the pot. And the frog is in the pot. And he says, jeez, actually, I'm, I'm comfortable. Water is getting a little bit warmer, but I am OK. And as the water heats up and heats up, all of a sudden, bit by bit, the frog realizes the water has gotten so hot that he can't jump out of the pot anymore. And this is the approach that the Germans did. They just built up the heat bit by bit by bit. First, everybody had to report. Then, it became necessary for everybody to wear a Jewish star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody had to report, or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody who was Jewish had to report. And they had to-- needed an Ausweis, a passport or identification papers. And the identification papers would show that you were a Jew. Because it had a J stamped on it. And then later on, you, you had to wear the yellow star. And then later on, all of a sudden--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember wearing the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, very distinctly, very distinctly-- because I remember in later years, and I'm talking living in Hawaii when I was in my-- maybe, like 10 years ago, I was the technical assistant to the play of And one of the girls-- and I'm moving way ahead now to give you an explanation on that yellow star-- one of the girls that was doing the detailing for the dress, for the dressing and how to dress the people, walked up to me. And she said, I copied this from an, from an information book. And she laid in front of me the yellow star I had worn. It was like somebody, all of a sudden, hit me with a two-by-four in the face. And I literally went-- yes. Yes, yes. I, I remember. Yeah, yeah, yeah. And I wanted to be professional about this. And I wanted to keep my emotion out of this whole thing and caught myself. But the first reaction-- I hadn't seen anything like that since, since the war. And when I saw this, it totally, it completely threw me, threw me back physically almost a couple of steps. I did a couple of steps backwards to, to absorb this, this impact of this Jewish star that I had worn. And maybe later on, I can explain to you why it meant so much to me, because I was wearing it for many-- for, for quite a while. Anyhow, we'll come to that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first day that you had to wear it, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not particularly, except that my mother did a good job in sewing stars on different outer garments that I would be wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be the reaction of people when you walked down the street and they saw the Star of David on your coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In general, there was no reactions. There, there was no reactions in general. Nobody ever said anything to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you to wear this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my, my character is very accepting and phlegmatic to a certain degree. And I felt, OK, if that's what I have to do. As a little kid, what are you going to say? So I said, well we have to wear this. So, all right, now, I just accepted it without any, any argument. Because I knew that I could not argue about the matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had non-Jewish friends. Actually, at that age, I never looked at friends whether they were Jewish or non-Jewish. It was totally unimportant for me at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they react to you wearing this yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, there were situations where I was being-- I remember there was one situation where some kids were kind of, yeah, yeah, yeah, you're a Jew, you're a Jew, you're-- and things like that, were hollering at me. And then one day-- and I told my father about that. And my father said, well, he said, I want to know more about that. And for the first time in his life-- and I was so proud of him-- he stood up for me. Because I kind of pulled them along close to where my father was. And then my father got out and tried to beat one of the kids up. But the kids were way too fast for him, so he couldn't catch any. After that, I never had any more troubles of anybody trying to jeer at me about being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first of all, you told us you had to sign up. Did you go-- did you-- do you remember you go to an office?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. My, my father did that. Because I, I was a, a six-year-old kid at that time-- six, seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't get any papers because you were too--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As a matter of fact, my mother was on my father's papers also. My mother didn't have her own papers. Yeah. It was just a time of tension. And you were always looking over your shoulders. You, you didn't know what the Germans were going to do next. That, that was the whole thing about this. And so we, we were becoming fearful. And the, the level of fear, the magnitude of fear, was starting to build up in everybody's life. That, that was the point that was taking place. The, the, the fear started to penetrate us. And because of the fear, we went underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we-- first we went, my father and I then-- to recap where we left off-- we went to a place on [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]. I just remembered the number now, 42, [INAUDIBLE] 42. And we went to, to this place, right upstairs. Doesn't exist anymore because there's a banking building there. It was across of a restaurant and bar.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went upstairs. And it was in the evening. And my father said, OK, let's, let's go to bed. And we went to bed. I took off most of my clothes because it was getting very hot. And that is it. And we stayed there for, oh, about a couple of months-- no, about five or six weeks, and didn't do anything. We just sat around. And then my-- finally, my mother and my sister decided to join us. Because we heard rumors all over the place that people were being taken away by the Nazis and to places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nobody knew where they went, until a later time when they found out that it was-- they went to a concentration camp called Westerbork. And I, I I guess that's how the, the, the fear started to penetrate us more and more and more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. Do, do we stop here at the moment and break for, for our next, next tape?
